ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific hazelnut milk tea entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers tea-based beverages with milk; no hazelnut-specific subcode exists. Closest defensible code used.

allergens: milk (non-dairy creamer may contain milk derivatives)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of hazelnut milk tea without topping, approximately 350 ml (350 g), as commonly served at Singaporean bubble tea and café outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Hazelnut Milk Tea (No Topping) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hazelnut Milk Tea (No Topping), cr_id D08010047, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — sugar-sweetened milk tea analogue
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hazelnut milk tea. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened milk tea / sugar-sweetened tea beverages, which report GI values in the range 60–70. The dominant glycaemic driver is sucrose from hazelnut syrup (11.06 g sugar/100 g). GL per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (38.7 g for 350 g serve) / 100 = 25.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (11.06 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 13 mg/100 g but does not offset the sugar-driven risk.
hypertension.guidance: People with hypertension should avoid this beverage in its standard form; request 0% sugar and substitute non-dairy creamer with low-fat fresh milk to reduce both sugar load and saturated fat from palm-based creamer.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.06 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes with no fibre-mediated attenuation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage; if consumed, request 0% sugar, replace hazelnut syrup with a sugar-free alternative, and substitute non-dairy creamer with low-fat milk to reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Non-dairy creamer contributes approximately 2.1 g saturated fat and 0.4 g trans fat per 100 g; combined with high added sugar (11.06 g/100 g), this beverage raises LDL cholesterol and promotes endothelial inflammation relevant to CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should avoid this beverage due to trans fat from non-dairy creamer and high added sugar; substituting fresh low-fat milk and requesting 0% sugar would substantially reduce CVD risk from this drink.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hazelnut Milk Tea (No Topping) is a freshly prepared hot or iced beverage sold at Singaporean bubble tea shops, café chains, and kopitiams. It is made by brewing black tea and combining it with non-dairy creamer and hazelnut-flavoured syrup, served without any additional toppings such as pearls, jelly, or pudding. The drink has a sweet, nutty hazelnut aroma layered over a mild tea base, with a creamy mouthfeel contributed by the non-dairy creamer. Per 100 g, it provides 65 kcal, 0.33 g protein, 2.41 g fat (of which approximately 2.1 g is saturated fat from palm-based non-dairy creamer and approximately 0.4 g is trans fat), 11.06 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and 13 mg sodium. The high sugar content (11.06 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard formulation. The absence of dietary fibre means there is no modulation of the postprandial glucose response. The trans fat from non-dairy creamer is a recognised pro-inflammatory and atherogenic factor. A standard takeaway serving of approximately 350 ml delivers around 227 kcal and 38.7 g of sugar. Clinically, this beverage should be consumed only occasionally and with sugar reduction requested. It is commonly consumed at breakfast or as an afternoon snack in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010047) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea, clearly documented.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — this beverage contains no fibre-contributing ingredients. 2. trans_fat_g (0.4 g/100 g) is estimated based on typical non-dairy creamer composition; HPB source does not separately report trans fat — this value is derived from published non-dairy creamer composition data and is flagged as estimated. saturated_fat_g (2.1 g/100 g) is similarly estimated from non-dairy creamer composition as HPB reports total fat only. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this specific beverage formulation is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea beverages; no direct GI measurement for hazelnut milk tea exists in published literature. GL per 350 g serving = 65 × 38.71 / 100 = 25.2. 5. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301099 (milk tea) and FoodEx2 A0FJP (tea-based beverages with milk) are closest defensible matches; no hazelnut milk tea-specific codes exist in published ontology releases as of 2026. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine category: English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
